author: Sofia Martínez
title: Using AI to Monitor Local Competitors: What They're Spending, Saying, Ranking
description: We built a competitive intelligence system for 12 service businesses using AI scraping and analysis. Here's what we found and how you can replicate it.

sections.p: Last month, we discovered that a competitor HVAC company in Charlotte was running 47 different Google Ads variations and spending roughly $8,000–$12,000 monthly based on ad frequency and click patterns. We knew this not through spyware but through legal, scalable AI monitoring tools that track public ad libraries, review sentiment, website changes, and pricing signals. We rebuilt our client's ad strategy in response, increased their budget 22%, and captured 9 additional jobs in six weeks. This is AI competitive intelligence in practice: automated insights that drive action.

sections.h2: What You Can Monitor (Legally and Easily)

sections.p: Google Ads Library, Meta Ad Library, and LinkedIn Campaign updates are public. Tools like Semrush, Brandwatch, and new AI-native platforms like Clay and Perplexity Pro can scrape and analyze this data at scale. You can track: competitor ad creatives, keyword targets, estimated budget (within 20–30% accuracy), landing page copy changes, promotion timing, and audience targeting signals.

We set up keyword tracking for a roofing company in Austin. Within 30 days, we identified that their main competitor was bidding heavily on "emergency roof leak repair" but their landing page was focused on new roof installation—a mismatch. We flagged that opportunity. Our client built a dedicated landing page for emergency repairs and captured 12 leads the competitor was losing. Cost of monitoring: $49/month. Value: one extra job per month, roughly $4,500 revenue.

list: Ad copy testing frequency: how often competitors change messaging (signals market response)
Review velocity: new 5-star vs. negative reviews weekly (shows customer satisfaction trends)
Website updates: keyword shifts, new service pages, pricing changes (indicates strategic pivots)
Seasonal spend patterns: when competitors increase budget (tells you peak demand periods)
Geographic expansion: new ad targeting areas (competitive threat early warning)

sections.h2: The AI Tools That Actually Work for Local Markets

sections.p: Semrush and Ahrefs give you domain-level competitive data: backlinks, organic rankings, traffic estimates. But for local service businesses, they're overkill. You need hyper-local, real-time monitoring. We use three tools in combination: (1) Google Ads Library + manual monthly review (free), (2) Clay for enrichment and contact data scraping ($99–$199/month), and (3) Brandwatch or Sprout Social for review and social sentiment tracking ($99–$249/month).

The real win comes from AI interpretation of that data, not collection. A locksmith company we work with ran all competitor data through Claude with this prompt: "Analyze these 15 competitors' ad strategies, review sentiment, and pricing. What are they emphasizing that we're not? Where's the gap?" Claude identified that 11 of 15 competitors were emphasizing 24/7 availability but none mentioned licensing or background checks—trust signals. We reframed the client's ads and landing page around "Licensed, bonded, background-checked locksmiths." Click-through rate increased 34%; lead quality improved 19%.

sections.quote: You don't need to outspend competitors. You need to notice what they're not doing and do it first.

sections.h2: Pricing Intelligence: The Hidden Data Source

sections.p: Most service businesses don't list pricing online. But competitors who do—or who mention price signals in ads—reveal market rate. We built a simple AI workflow: scrape competitor websites weekly, extract any mention of "$" pricing, standardize the data, and alert when pricing changes. A pest control company discovered competitors were bundling an extra treatment monthly at no upcharge during summer. They hadn't done that before. We added it to their offer within two weeks, and upsell rate climbed 28%.

For markets where pricing is opaque, this signal is crucial. A plumbing company in Portland discovered that 8 of 12 competitors offered a free diagnostic visit. They didn't. We tested the free diagnostic offer as an ad hook, and it drove 31% more call volume—same budget, better targeting mechanism.

list: Set up weekly scraping of competitor website homepages and service pages (use Clay or Make.com automation)
Extract and log any pricing, guarantees, or promotional language (2 min per week)
Feed monthly summaries into Claude or Gemini with simple analysis prompt (5 min)
Act on one insight per month: test it in your own campaigns (30 days), measure, scale or abandon)
Cost per insight: roughly $10–$15 in tool spend; ROI typically 400–800% within 60 days

sections.p: You don't need a complex system. We use a Google Sheet updated monthly with: competitor name, estimated monthly ad spend (range), top 5 keywords they bid on, average review rating, newest reviews sentiment (positive/neutral/negative), last major website update date, and one strategic observation. Takes 45 minutes monthly per competitor. Cross-reference with your own data, and patterns emerge.

One of our clients, a cleaning service, noticed their top competitor increased ad spend 60% in March. The dashboard flagged it. We investigated and found they'd hired a marketing person and were testing new service combos. We monitored their results, saw what stuck (deep-clean packages), and copied the offer to our client's business within four weeks. We were second to market but not third. That timing difference—being first-to-act versus late—typically drives 15–25% higher conversion on new offers.
